speaker: interviewer
text: --four, survivor Bronia Sonnenschein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Sonnenschein, can you describe what it felt like when you went on a death march?

speaker: interviewee
text: I won't forget it. After three months Dresden, and witnessing the greatest air attack on Dresden by the Allies, February, 1945, we were again assembled and told to line up. At this moment, when we were lining up, a German woman-- she was, I suppose, the housekeeper in that factory-- she knew my face, not my name-- I didn't have a name-- because I worked in the office again. And she came repeatedly to the office to have work done, so she knew my face. And she came up to me, and she made sure the guards don't see her. And she thrust a letter into my hand-- I'm having it here. And she said, take it, believe in it. It will protect you. I don't think it took more than a second or two, we had-- she had to watch. I put it in my blouse, and I couldn't even say thank you. And I didn't know what was in the letter-- not till the day I was liberated and could really read it. The death march nearly took two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's backtrack just for a second. When you received that letter you couldn't open, were you curious what is in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You know, it was given to me-- our strength was ebbing. It was given to me-- everybody wanted me to die. That was their intention. If they don't kill us, we'll die anyway. She, for some reason-- only god in heaven will know-- wanted me to live. She didn't even know my name. That's why she said, believe it, it will protect you. And from this one sentence, she gave me hope. I never had a ray of hope. By that time, it was pretty well down.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you finally opened up that letter and read it, can you tell us in a short few words what was in it, that this woman wanted you to have, and you believed so strongly, even if you didn't see the letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I opened it. It is called a schutzbrief in German. [? 'Schutz' ?] is to protect you, to help you. And it is a Christian letter. I didn't care if she wanted me to live, because it says, in the name of the Father, the Son, and the Holy Ghost, Amen. And refers to the Christian belief, but it also has a lot of the Ten Commandments in it. And it was found in Germany in the year-- and it was found in Germany in the year 1700. That's what I finally was reading. And it continuously said in order that it will protect you, you have to believe in the power of this letter. So you see, before I read it, just because she told me that it will protect me if I believe in it, I believed in it without having laid eyes on it. And I carried the letter with me through the death march that lasted nearly two weeks, through liberation, and my struggle to stand on my own feet. Today, April-- January 23, 1996, I have my letter. And I continue to believe in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went on a death march, can you describe what it was like? How did you walk? Did you have any food, water? Where did you go? Did you know where you're going?

speaker: interviewee
text: We never knew where they take us. No. That was all the time unknown. As to food, we got maybe twice a slice of bread. On the street are no kitchens-- there's no soup. We didn't get water either. At one point, I was so desperately thirsty, I must have lost my mind when I approached a guard. The Austrians have a different accent, like I have a different accent speaking German than the German have.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I approached him and asked him, please, may I have a little bit of water? To do that, I can assure you, I must have lost my mind. He gave me one look-- he was big guy-- stretched out his hand, and slapped me in the face that I can feel the sting almost. That was his answer. But I wasn't thirsty anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And how we walked-- I concentrated, putting one foot in front of the other. We drank the water-- we walked always along the river Elbe, E-L-B-E, that flows through Germany-- the same river where we tossed the bodies of people that collapsed on the street and died. And two guards came. One grabbed the corpse by the head-- by the feet, one by the head-- and tossed them into the river. That's where we could relieve ourselves. That was the water they let us drink. And at some point on the death march-- we still walked side by side-- I was so swollen, my legs were so swollen, my stomach was swollen, my whole face was swollen. And so was [? Paola. ?] My mother became like a toothpick-- so thin.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was towards the end, as it turned out, of the death march that Paola approached me. And she whispered, I cannot walk anymore. Why don't we finish it? Why don't the three of us go into the river?

speaker: interviewee
text: And [INAUDIBLE] was so full of life, I had agree with her. I knew I couldn't much longer. And I whispered it-- we always [INAUDIBLE] nobody watching-- my mother. And this is the reason because I'm here. Because my mother said. Swear to you that is the reason. She overheard the guard mentioning a date, so she knew the exact date. And she said, tomorrow is Paola's birthday. Paola was my beloved sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's wait one more day. Maybe a miracle will happen. And so we waited one more day.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on this day, we arrived at the ghetto of Theresienstadt. I didn't know a ghetto in Theresienstadt existed. But that's where the guard left us, and they were running away because it was close to the end of the war, which we didn't know either. And the very few Jews-- it was called the model ghetto, for the world to see how well we were treated. And they seen us marching in. It was called the march of the death because we were like dying people. And they had in their hand-- outstretched hands-- cubed sugar. I don't know how many pieces of cube sugar I ate because they said it makes the heart beat again. And so we ate the cube sugar. And then they took us to showers-- real showers-- warm water, soap, and a towel. And they burned our clothes. I forgot to mention, all through the time in camp-- Auschwitz, everywhere-- we were covered with lice. Covered with lice. They burned the clothes and they gave us fresh, clean-- also rags, but it was clean. And I had underwear. And then they gave us the best soup I maybe ate in my life. Warm soup. And they led us to the barracks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was the first night-- no beating, no fear-- that we could sleep. And on May 8th, a young Russian soldier-- he was maybe 18 years young-- 18 years old-- younger than I was. He came to the camp on a horse. And we dragged ourselves out. My mother was really failing. And we supported each other. And we looked up at him, and people came up to him and kissed his boots. He was the messenger of peace. He came and said, you're free. You can go home now. There was no home to go to, but I was free.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he say that in German or Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said it in Russian. But I had learned some Polish in four years in the ghetto. When he said, [RUSSIAN], I knew what he meant-- home. And [RUSSIAN]-- I knew it was 'free,' because it sounded like Polish. I knew what he said.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were there in that--

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not know-- --Theresienstadt. --Victoria. Countless people died on the death march. Countless people broke down and died by approaching Theresienstadt. And there were so few of us that heard him say that we are free.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once you heard that word, 'freedom,' what did go through your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like everybody here had watched V-day, end of the war. And you have seen confetti, and jubilation, and dancing on the street, and kissing each other-- there was none of it. You see, we had lost the capacity to cry. We never cried. But we-- when we heard that [INAUDIBLE] are free, we all cried. Tears were flowing. An ocean of tears.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think, where are you to go now?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I savored the word 'free.' I was an equal among equals again. You understand that went through my mind. I was human, not subhuman. And I know if God led me this far, I will make it. I was hospitalized, like almost everybody. I had [INAUDIBLE], mostly on my hands, that had to be bandaged. Paula had it on her chest. Everybody had it. We were total malnutrition. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I knew I will get better. And I knew we came so far, the three of us. That there is people in the world. There was hope now. There was finally real hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, there is left your mother, your sister, and you. The liberation came. What happened after that? Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Prague, after I was dismissed from the hospital, like all of us. There was also quarantine because there was typhus in the camp, obviously. We never, thank god, had that. We went to Prague. It was very close to Theresienstadt. And my first husband had told me when we were told to leave the ghetto, if something happens we will-- let's say we both survive-- we will meet in Prague. He had a close friend with whom he went to university together, who was married to a non-Jewish woman, so he figured he might be safe. And he gave me the address. I memorized it. And that was my first step, to knock on that man's door. And luckily for me, he spoke German, because I didn't speak a word of Czech either. And I only told him I was [INAUDIBLE] wife. I couldn't believe that he would believe me, the way I looked. Still in rags-- clean, but in rags. Hair was still short. But he believed me. And slowly-- slowly-- I started to work in a factory where I put vanilla powder into little packages, together with my mother, on the assembly line. I was able-- in the beginning, I had nowhere to go to sleep. A convent took me in. We had mattresses-- we were given mattresses and a sheet, and we could sleep. And in order to have a shower-- finally, a shower again-- we could go to train stations. And they gave us food, as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you say you went to convent and got a shelter.

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- not a shelter. It was a real, down-to-earth convent. They took in people like us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not remember the name. I talked very little. I didn't speak Czech. But all I said-- I asked, you know, Jewish koncentracni camp. You know, it sounds like Polish. And they took us in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long you stayed in Prague after liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Prague? I stayed in Prague four years. I got-- after-- I worked side by side with my mother in the factory with the baking powder--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your sister at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Paola had also gone through a so-called marriage a week before we were deported. And she-- somebody had told her in Prague that her husband was killed. She didn't believe it. He was born in Lodz and he was from Lodz, Poland. He used to work in the-- was a fireman in the ghetto. She went there and-- because that's what he said. It's where they will try to meet. And there was no note from him. But she found out-- I don't know how. Somebody said he was in Bergen-Belsen. He was liberated, actually, in Bergen-Belsen. And so Paola went-- you know, hitchhiked part of the way; nobody had money-- to Bergen-Belsen. He was told that Paola was not alive. So when she stood there, he thought he'd died all over again. And so they were reunited. And I continued to work in the factory. And, pure chance, I was offered a job through a friend that I knew-- she had told me about it-- in an export firm. And I applied for the job. That lawyer's wife-- she was exactly my height, my age-- she gave me one of her dresses. And shoes-- we had the same size. And I felt all dressed up. And I applied for the job, and I got it. I did the foreign correspondence-- German, English, and French. And I became a real somebody again that was given responsibility. And I could stand on her own feet, and I could tell my mother, no more work for you. I have enough money for both of us. And we rented a room with kitchen privileges, and we lived in Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: For four years?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1948-- no, in 1947, communists took over. And it was all over again. It was all over again. Like under Nazis, now it was the Stalin regime. People disappeared from the street. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were afraid again. I lost the job and applied in the American Joint Distribution, a Jewish organization helping Jews to immigrate. I applied for a job and was welcomed with open arms. By that time, I had learned some Czech, so I spoke some Czech, some Polish, German, English, some French. So I had a job. And when I started my first day on the job, I was assigned to the-- there were different-- you know, there was immigration to America, to Australia, to Canada. I was told I will work with a Dr. Kurt Sonnenschein, in the place, you know, where we organized immigration to United States. And that was my husband-to-be then.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's how you met your second husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's how I met my second husband. And I had taken one look at him. I barely-- I just knew his name by then, because they told me. But I knew that I wanted to marry him. I wanted nothing so much, except children, and I could see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So with first husband, you did not have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. The children wouldn't have been alive. I wouldn't have been alive. No, no, no, nothing. And so we worked together, hard, in the American Joint. I mean-- I didn't marry him the next day. It's not a movie. But I was very much attracted to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: By that time, I was, inside, healed. You understand? Inside. It was not only my body-- my mind had begun to heal. And even in the misery, I wanted to live.

speaker: interviewee
text: But now I was free. I could work. I could hold up my head high. I really wanted to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you immigrate to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: We met in August, 1948. In December-- December 28th, 1948, we were born-- we were married. And the door was still not wide open to the free world. But by that time, we had a country of our own-- Israel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We emigrated to Israel. And by that time, I was expecting my first baby. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We arrived in Haifa, Israel, and on December 8th, 1949, our son was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: The son that you have now here?

speaker: interviewee
text: The son that I have now. The son who is my best friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name is Dan?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Dan, D-A-N. Was my greatest support. I couldn't do what I'm doing without both my children's encouragement and support.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you have your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter-- we came to Canada. It was a bad time in Israel. There was, again, not enough food. It wasn't the Israel that is today. There was an influx of immigrants, and Israel was so small and so poor. And I didn't want to be poor again and not enough food. And so we finally managed to get to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you stay in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: 10 months. So when my son was four months old, we left. We stayed a month with my late husband's friend in [INAUDIBLE]. A month in New York, where we had the reunion with my relatives that had gotten out in time. And then we came-- July, 1950, we came to Vancouver, with no money, nothing. Nothing. But we had each other. In July, 1951, my daughter-- our daughter, Vivian, was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Vivian-- this is your daughter. And what is her last name now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Herman, H-E-R-M-A-N. She was born in Vancouver, and [INAUDIBLE] family that was complete. And my mother lived with us. And everything-- my husband worked very, very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband had a degree in law-- a PhD in law. Yet when he came here, all he could get was a job on the [INAUDIBLE] in a mill-- lumber. He really couldn't do physical work. And so, thank god, he was given, in the same company, an office job. So things really started to look bright. I hoped when then the children got school age-- my mother lived with me-- I will be able to work and we will-- we might get ahead. I was able to get my sister here after a year in Vancouver, and her husband, Stan. And so I had my beautiful family all together, plus a husband, plus my children. And then everything looked like peaches and cream. We were taken on an outing on September 1st, 1952. My daughter was 14 months old. Dan was 2 and 1/2. We were taken to an outing to Squamish, only we never made it. A few blocks where we lived, [? where ?] we rented apartment, was a head-on collision. We were all thrown out of the car, even our little boy. And on the 3rd of September, without regaining consciousness, my husband died. And again, there was no goodbye.

speaker: interviewer
text: And ever since, you lived on your own, raising your own family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Raised my children-- with the help of my mother and Paola, who worshiped my children. And her husband, who stepped in to be a father figure-- her husband. And, you know, God had helped me all along the way. The grief of losing my husband after being only married four years, after having plans that everything will be so good. The future looked so-- I was so confident. I was, again, face this tremendous sorrow. And, on top of it, no money. We were only two years in the country. I was injured, too-- fractured skull, broken ribs, and the tremendous, tremendous shock of losing my husband. But I pulled myself up. And I was given a job in the office where my husband had worked. And I worked on the job for 25 years. And when my daughter got married and expected her first baby, she said, enough, Mom. We don't-- you don't have to work for us anymore. We can make it on our own. I'm married now. Dan could do it on his own.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop now and change the tape, and we'll come back.
